content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance-Administration • • • Executive-Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660          322-5961          322-6441          322-6444

December 1, 1977

77-12-699

Henry J. Vaux
Chairman
State Board of Forestry
1416 9th Street
Sacramento, California   95814

Dear Mr. Vaux:

     Thank you for your letter of November 8, 1977, requesting informal advice with respect to the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act. First, you asked whether the general disqualification language contained in Government Code Section 87100 applies to the Board of Forestry prior to that Board's adoption of a Conflict of Interest Code.1/  Section 87100 provides:

     No public official at any level of state or local government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest.

     The provisions of Section 87100 are applicable to all public officials, both state and local, who make governmental decisions irrespective of whether such officials are subject to conflict of interest codes.

     The Commission has further defined the term "public official" by Regulation, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(a)(1), which provides

________________
1/  All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.

Henry J. Vaux
December 1, 1977
Page Two


in relevant part:

     (a)  "Public official at any level of state or
local government" means every natural person who
is a member, officer, employee or consultant of
a state or local government agency.

     (1)  "Member" shall include, but not be
limited to, salaried or unsalaried members of
boards or commissions with decision-making
authority.  A board or commission possesses
decision-making authority whenever:

          (A)  It may make a final governmental
decision;

          (B)  It may compel a governmental
decision; or it may prevent a governmental
decision either by reason of an exclusive
power to initiate the decision or by reason
of a veto which may not be overridden; or

          (C)  It makes substantive recommendations
which are, and over an extended period of time
which have been, regularly approved without signifi-
cant amendment or modification by another
public official or governmental agency.

     Under Public Resources Code Sections 740 and 4551, et seq.,
the State Board of Forestry is charged and empowered to adopt
Rules and Regulations imposing restraints and setting conditions
and standards for the harvesting of timber.  We think it clear
that the quasi-legislative rule-making power conferred by
these sections constitutes the making of a final governmental
decision within the meaning of the cited Commission Regulation.
Accordingly, members of the Board of Forestry are subject to
the general prohibitions of Section 87100, et seq.

     As you suggest in your letter, Board members may be subject
to other provisions of California law concerning the subject of
conflicts of interest.  This is specifically permitted by Govern-
ment Code Section 81013 which states:

Henry J. Vaux
December 1, 1977
Page Three


     Nothing in this title prevents the Legislature
or any other state or local agency from imposing
additional requirements on any person if the
requirements do not prevent the person from
complying with this title. If any act of the
Legislature conflicts with the provisions of this
title, this title shall prevail.

     Second, you asked whether the non-public members of the
Board will be disqualified from voting on District Forest Practice
Rules. Public Resources Section 731 provides that three of the
non-public members shall be selected from the forest product
industry and one member from the range livestock industry.
Assuming that the Conflict of Interest Code ultimately adopted
for the State Board of Forestry incorporates the regulations of
the Fair Political Practices Commission, disqualification of
"industry representatives" will be governed by 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18703(d) which provides in pertinent part:

     A material financial effect on a governmental
decision on an official's interests, as described
in Government Code Section 87103(a) through (d),
is distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally unless the decision will affect the
official's interest in substantially the same
manner as it will affect all members of the public
or a significant segment of the public. Except as
provided herein, an industry, trade or profession
does not constitute a significant segment of the
general public.

     (d) Through January 1, 1979, an industry, trade
or profession constitutes a significant segment of
the public if the statute, ordinance or other
provision of law which creates or authorizes
the creation of the official's agency contains
a requirement or express authorization that
members of that industry, trade or profession
holds such office.

     Thus, for the purposes of participating in decisions of the
State Board of Forestry, forest products industry and the range
livestock industry are deemed significant segments of the public
generally prior to January 1, 1979. Accordingly, non-public
members of the Board may make or participate in decisions which
will foreseeably and materially affect those industries so long

Henry J. Vaux
December 1, 1977
Page Four


as such decisions affect the member's interest in substantially
the same manner as they affect other members of the industry.
In the instant case, it is our understanding that all District
Forest Rules will apply equally to all persons in the District
in question. Therefore, in the absence of circumstances suggesting that a particular Board decision will be favorable or
harmful to the official's interest as opposed to other interests
within the industry in question, we conclude that non-public
members may make and participate in the making of Board decisions
relating to District Forest Rules.

     Third, you ask whether a Board member who works for or owns
a company that operates in the Coastal Zone or who represents
employees or management in such company will be required to
disqualify himself or herself from voting on board regulations
or special treatment areas in the Coastal Zone. Public Resources
Code Section 30118.5 defines special treatment areas as follows:

     "Special treatment area" means an identifiable
and geographically bound forested area within the
coastal zone that constitutes a significant habitat
area, area of special scenic significance, and
any land where logging activities could adversely
affect public recreation area or the biological
productivity of any wetland, estuary, or stream
especially valuable because of its role in a
coastal ecosystem.

     As discussed above in the context of District Forest Rules,
industry representatives may vote on regulations which will
foreseeably and materially affect the industries which such
members represent so long as the governmental decision in
question will affect all members of the industry equally.
Therefore, industry representatives may vote on regulations
creating special treatment areas so long as such regulations
do not affect the official's interest in a manner different
from the effect on other interests within the forest products
industry and range livestock industry.

     If you have any further questions, please contact me or
Ted Prim, Chief of the Conflicts of Interest Division.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]

                                        Alfredo Flores
                                        Research Specialist I
                                        Conflicts of Interest Division

AF:TF:KWG:mfa

RESOURCES AGENCY                                                    EDMUND G. BROWN JR.
                                                                              GOVERNOR




                            State of California

                          Board of Forestry
                              1416 NINTH STREET
                          SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814

                              November 8, 1977


Mr. Ted Prim, Chief
Conflict of Interest Section
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA  95814

Dear Mr. Prim:

     Pursuant to 2 Cal. Admin. Code 18329 and Government Code Section
8314(b) of the Fair Political Reform Act of 1974, the State Board
of Forestry wishes to request your informal advice.

     The Department of Forestry is in the process of developing a
Conflict of Interest Code.  In our review of the Code, a major
concern has developed over the potential impacts on Board delibera-
tions of the disqualification elements of the Fair Political
Practices Act.  We would consequently like to clarify the possible
impact of the disqualification language on our Board.

     Three basic questions are of concern.

     1.   What is the impact on the Board of the general disqualification
          language contained in the Fair Political Practices Act prior
          to formal adoption of a Conflict of Interest Code?

     2.   What will be the impact on the Board of Article 5, "Disqualifi-
          cation" of the Conflict of Interest Code proposed by the
          Department on the voting of non-public members on District
          Forest Practice Rules?

     3.   More specifically, at a December 1977 hearing, will a Board
          member who works for or owns a company that operates in
          the Coastal Zone, or who represents employees or management
          of such a company, be required to disqualify himself or
          herself from voting on Board regulations for Special Treatment
          Areas within the Coastal Zone?

     To aid your analysis in these areas, some background information
is provided on each of these questions.

     With respect to question 1, the Board presently operates under
Section 737 of the Public Resources Code, which requires a Board
member to disqualify himself when he has a direct, personal,
financial interest in a Board decision within the meaning of
Section 1120 of the Government Code.  We would like to know the

Mr. Ted Prim
November 8, 1977
Page 2

impact of the Fair Political Practices Act on Section 737 of the
Public Resources Code, particularly since a conflict of interest
code has not been formally adopted. In particular, is a different
standard of disqualification required and, if so, must it be
implemented immediately?

With respect to question 2, PRC Sections 730 and 731 establish
within the Department a mine member Board of Forestry. The Board
is composed of five members of the general public, three members
from the forest products industry, and one from the range livestock
industry. Sections 740-745 of the Public Resources Code sets forth
general responsibilities of the Board.

Section 4551 of the Public Resources Code further requires the
Board to adopt District Forest Practice Rules and Regulations
to guide timber harvesting regulations. These regulations impose
constraints on the harvesting operations for the purpose of protecting soil, water, wildlife, etc. Without a doubt, the regulations
add to the cost of harvesting operations and hence, impact the
income which the timber owners derive from logging.

The State is divided into three forest practice districts, each
with a number of timber companies and numerous small timber
growers. The estimated acreage and ownership in these districts is
indicated below:

                                    Coast           Northern        Southern

Total Timberland               3.8 mill. ac.   9.5 mill. ac.   3.0 mill. ac.
  Private                        2.9             3.8             0.8
  Public                         0.9             5.7             2.2

Acres held by large
  companies                      .94             1.9             640,000 ac.

No. large companies              66              44              30

Acres held by small
  landowners                     1.96            1.9             160,000 ac.

No. small owners                14,000          14,000          2,000

In addition to statewide forest practice rules, the Board promulgates
rules and regulations that are specific to each district. Rules
vary between districts to allow for the variety of forest types and
harvesting conditions found throughout the state.

In connection with promulgation of District Forest Practice Rules,
the Board is concerned about the disqualification section of
the Department of Forestry's proposed Conflict of Interest Code.
Non-public members of the Board of Forestry may be individual
timberland owners or employees of timber companies which operate
within a single forest practice district. In some cases, Board

Mr. Ted Prim
November 8, 1977
Page 3

members are faced with making decisions on district rules which
may cost themselves or their firms a significant amount of money.
However, as the rules are uniform across the district, the financial
effect of meeting the rules is similar on all companies within the
region. In such a case, would Board members need to disqualify
themselves?

With respect to question 3, the California Coastal Act of 1976
requires the Board of Forestry to promulgate rules to protect
various values in special treatment areas on private land contained
within boundaries of the Coastal Commission. The Board will be
holding a hearing in early December to consider adoption of special
rules for these areas. Special rules will probably impose extra
cost on owners harvesting timber within the zone. Several Board
members own, work for, or are closely associated with companies
that have land or harvest timber in the Coastal Zone and will,
therefore, be affected by these special regulations. It is
estimated that there are 100,000 to 200,000 acres of timberland
held by at least six companies and numerous small owners in the
region. Will the above-mentioned Board members need to disqualify?

The Forest Practice Act expressly requires that four members of
the Board be selected from specific economic interests affected by
regulation of forest practices. The reason for this requirement
appears to reflect the Legislature's view that valuable expertise and
policy perspectives necessary to long-term protection of the public
interest in forest land, can best be reflected in the Board's actions
by representation drawn from economic interests that are identified
with such expertise and policy perspective. It is the Board's view
that strict application of the rule proposed in 14 C.A.C. Section
805 would disqualify four of the nine members of the Board from
participation in most of the significant rule-making activities
and many of the quasi-judicial activities of the Board and that
the result would be an effective repeal by the Fair Political
Practices Act of the Z'berg-Nejedly Forest Practice Act, a consequence that seems unlikely to have been anticipated by the Electorate
when Proposition Nine was adopted, and which was not discussed in
any of the ballot pamphlets in connection with that election.

Inasmuch as your response may have a significant effect on how
the Board operates, we would appreciate as rapid a response as
possible. If further details are needed, please contact Board
of Forestry staff at 445-2921 or 445-2753.

Sincerely,

Henry J. Vaux, Chairman

HJV:DC:gk

sections.question: 1. What is the impact on the Board of the general disqualification language contained in the Fair Political Practices Act prior to formal adoption of a Conflict of Interest Code? 2. What will be the impact on the Board of Article 5, "Disqualification" of the Conflict of Interest Code proposed by the Department on the voting of non-public members on District Forest Practice Rules? 3. More specifically, at a December 1977 hearing, will a Board member who works for or owns a company that operates in the Coastal Zone, or who represents employees or management of such a company, be required to disqualify himself or herself from voting on Board regulations for Special Treatment Areas within the Coastal Zone?
sections.question_synthetic: The State Board of Forestry seeks FPPC guidance on how the Fair Political Practices Act's disqualification provisions apply to board members with financial interests in timber operations, particularly regarding District Forest Practice Rules and Coastal Zone regulations. The Board is concerned that strict application of disqualification rules could effectively prevent industry representatives from participating in regulatory decisions despite statutory requirements for their representation.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter, not an FPPC response. The document contains three detailed questions regarding disqualification requirements but no CONCLUSION or FPPC advice. Letter dated November 8, 1977, from the Board of Forestry to Ted Prim at the FPPC requesting guidance on conflict of interest code implications.

What will be the impact on the Board of Article 5, "Disqualification" of the Conflict of Interest Code proposed by the Department on the voting of non-public members on District Forest Practice Rules? 3. More specifically, at a December 1977 hearing, will a Board member who works for or owns a company that operates in the Coastal Zone, or who represents employees or management of such a company, be required to disqualify himself or herself from voting on Board regulations for Special Treatment Areas within the Coastal Zone?

embedding.summary: This is an incoming request from the California State Board of Forestry to the FPPC seeking informal advice on the applicability and impact of conflict of interest disqualification provisions on board members who have financial interests in timber operations subject to the board's regulatory authority.
